coredata.dc:title: Light-addressable photoelectrochemical sensors for multichannel detections of GPC1, CEA and GSH and its applications in early diagnosis of pancreatic cancer

coredata.dc:description: Glypican-1 (GPC1) has been demonstrated to be a potential biomarker of pancreatic cancer, so ultrasensitive detection of GPC1 was important for the diagnosis of pancreatic cancer. In this paper, a photoelectrochemical (PEC) immunosensor based on gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) was developed for the first time for the ultrasensitive detection of GPC1. Then graphene oxide (GO) was introduced into AuNCs as a hybrid nanomaterial to improve the sensitivity. The results confirmed that GO increased the sensitivity by enhancing both the photoelectric properties and antibody loading properties. Finally, the PEC immunosensors for GPC1 detection achieved an optimal sensitivity (7.265 nA/decade) and an excellent limit of detection (LOD) of 15 pg/mL (S/N = 3). To provide more information for the early diagnosis of pancreatic cancer, multichannel light-addressable PEC sensors that achieved the simultaneous detection of biomacromolecules and biomicromolecules was further developed by AuNCs/GO based PEC immunosensors and carbon dots@mesoporous silica beads (CDs@MSB) based PEC sensor. The simultaneous ultrasensitive detection of GPC1, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and glutathione (GSH) were achieved by using this light-addressable PEC sensor. Finally, samples from pancreatic cancer models were detected, which verified the practicality and accuracy of the light-addressable PEC sensor in the evaluation and early diagnosis of pancreatic cancer.

Absorption spectra of (A) AuNCs and AuNCs/GO, (B) CDs and CDs@MSB. AFM images of (C) AuNCs (green circles)/GO (white circles) and (D) CDs@MSB.

(A) I-V curve of AuNCs/AuE in PBS solution. (B) The photoelectric conversion mechanism of AuNCs/GO/AuE PEC sensor. (C) Photocurrent response and (D) EIS of (a) AuNCs/AuE, (b) GO/AuE, (c) AuNCs/GO/AuE, (d) anti-GPC1/AuNCs/GO/AuE and (e) GPC1/anti-GPC1/AuNCs/GO/AuE in 0.1 M PBS (pH=7.4) at 500 mV. (E) Photocurrent responses to different concentrations of GPC1 (a) 0, (b) 0.01, (c) 0.05, (d) 0.1, (e) 0.5, (f) 1, (g) 5 ng/mL in 0.1 M PBS (pH=7.4). Inset: Plot of photocurrent change (ΔI) vs. concentration of GPC1.

(A) The reduction of photocurrent (ΔI) of the PEC immunosensor at different applied potentials in 0.1 M PBS containing 1 ng/mL GPC1 (the concentration of GO was 0.1 mg/mL). (B) The relationship between the GO concentration and the photocurrent of the AuNCs/GO/AuE. (C) The relationship between the sensitivity of PEC immunosensor and the concentration of GO at 500 mV. (D) The working curve of the PEC immunosensor for GPC1 detection under optimal conditions.

(A) The sensing mechanism of theCDs@MSB based PEC sensor for GSH detection. (B) The relationship between MSB concentration and photocurrent of the CDs@MBS based PEC sensor at + 500 mV. (C) The sensitivities of CDs@MBS based PEC sensors with different concentrations of MSB. (D) Photocurrent of CDs@MBS based PEC sensors in PBS containing different concentrations of GSH.
The photocurrent responses of (A) AuNCs/GO based immunosensor and (B) CDs@MSB based PEC sensor in PBS mixed with different concentrations of GPC1 and GSH at 500 mV.

The detection of (A) GPC1, (B) CEA and (C) GSH by multichannel PEC sensors in PANC-1 cells and the control group (different numbers of HDFs). The concentrations of (D) GPC1, (E) CEA and (F) GSH in PANC-1 cells and HDFs of different numbers measured by kits as references.

The detection of (A) GPC1, (B) CEA and (C) GSH by multichannel PEC sensors from mouse models with different culture times and control groups (mice cultured for the same days without injection of pancreatic cancer cells). (F) The reference results of (D) GPC1, (E) CEA and (F) GSH detected by kits.

The detection of (A) GPC1, (B) CEA and (C) GSH by multichannel PEC sensors in normal human serum samples (control group) and serum samples from two patients at early stages and two patients at advanced stages of pancreatic cancer. The detection results of GPC1 (D), CEA (E) and GSH (F) by kits from the same serum samples.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glypican-1 (GPC1) has been demonstrated to be a potential biomarker of pancreatic cancer, so ultrasensitive detection of GPC1 was important for the diagnosis of pancreatic cancer. In this paper, a photoelectrochemical (PEC) immunosensor based on gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) was developed for the first time for the ultrasensitive detection of GPC1. Then graphene oxide (GO) was introduced into AuNCs as a hybrid nanomaterial to improve the sensitivity. The results confirmed that GO increased the sensitivity by enhancing both the photoelectric properties and antibody loading properties. Finally, the PEC immunosensors for GPC1 detection achieved an optimal sensitivity (7.265 nA/decade) and an excellent limit of detection (LOD) of 15 pg/mL (S/N = 3). To provide more information for the early diagnosis of pancreatic cancer, multichannel light-addressable PEC sensors that achieved the simultaneous detection of biomacromolecules and biomicromolecules was further developed by AuNCs/GO based PEC immunosensors and carbon dots@mesoporous silica beads (CDs@MSB) based PEC sensor. The simultaneous ultrasensitive detection of GPC1, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and glutathione (GSH) were achieved by using this light-addressable PEC sensor. Finally, samples from pancreatic cancer models were detected, which verified the practicality and accuracy of the light-addressable PEC sensor in the evaluation and early diagnosis of pancreatic cancer.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of digestive tract malignant tumor, pancreatic cancer has the characteristics of low diagnosis rate, rapid deterioration and high mortality [1,2]. The clinical symptoms of patients with early pancreatic cancer are similar to those of pancreatitis and other related diseases, which makes it difficult to diagnose and thus easy to miss the best time for medical intervention, resulting in advanced pancreatic cancer [3]. The survival rate of advanced pancreatic cancer is only 3% [4]. Therefore, the early diagnosis and evaluation of pancreatic cancer is very important.

At present, the diagnosis of pancreatic cancer mainly depends on medical imaging modalities, including computed tomography (CT), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) [5–7]. These traditional diagnostic methods are frequently used to diagnose pancreatic cancer when patients present related symptoms. Medical imaging technology is highly accurate, but the detection processes are highly invasive, costly and make it difficult to screen for pancreatic cancer at the early stage [8–10]. As an emerging technique, blood-based liquid biopsy seemed to be a potential method of detecting pancreatic cancer in the early stage [11,12]. A blood-based liquid biopsy, which detects tumor biomarkers in the blood, is a noninvasive method with the advantages of convenient operation, low cost and real time detection. In previous studies, a few biomarkers related to pancreatic cancer have been identified, including carbohydrate antigen 19–9 (CA19–9), CA242, and carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) [13–15]. These tumor biomarkers have been widely used in pancreatic cancer diagnosis, but they have limitations such as low specificity and sensitivity. Hence, more reliable biomarkers are needed for diagnosis at early stage. In recent years, proteoglycan glypican-1 (GPC1), a membrane anchored protein, has been identified as a biomarker of pancreatic cancer. A number of studies have confirmed the high specificity of GPC1 as a biomarker in the early stage [16–18]. Therefore, the diagnosis of early pancreatic cancer can be achieved by ultrasensitive detection of the concentration of GPC1 in the blood.

To date, various immunoassay methods, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), optical methods and electrochemical methods have been widely used for biomarker detection in blood-based liquid biopsy. These methods realize quantitative detection of biomarkers, but they each have their own drawbacks [8,19–21]. The ELISA method is limited by low sensitivity, poor repeatability, complicated operation and susceptibility to the environment. Optical methods such as fluorescent immunoassay and chemiluminescence have a high sensitivity, but they are costly and require complicated pretreatment and sophisticated instruments. Although electrochemical methods only require relatively cheap equipment and simple operation, their low signal-to-noise ratio is not sufficient for effective and accurate early detection of pancreatic cancer. As an emerging sensing method, photoelectrochemical (PEC) methods use optical signals as excitation sources and detect electric output signals. Therefore, PEC methods combine the features of optical methods and electrochemical methods, showing the advantages of high sensitivity, low cost, simple operation and a high signal-to-noise ratio [22–24]. Moreover, PEC sensors can be easily expanded into light-addressable sensors for multichannel detection of multiple analytes. In recent years, nanomaterials have been widely used to fabricate PEC sensors due to their high reactivity [21,25,26]. To further improve their sensing performance, hybrid nanomaterials attracted much attention because of their excellent photoelectric conversion ability and catalytic activity [27,28].

Therefore, hybrid nanomaterial-based PEC sensors were used to achieve the ultrasensitive detection of GPC1 for the early diagnosis of pancreatic cancer in this work. Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) with 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid (MUA) ligands were modified with specific human glypican-1 antibody (anti-GPC1) and were used to fabricate a PEC sensor to achieve highly specific detection of GPC1. Then, GO was introduced to form AuNCs/GO nanocomposites to enhance the sensing performance.

Moreover, previous studies have shown that combined detection of multiple biomarkers, such as CEA and glutathione (GSH), can achieve better performance in the diagnosis of pancreatic cancer [29–33]. Previous combined detection of multiple biomarkers often analyzed each biomarker separately in different samples and cannot achieve the simultaneous detection of biomacromolecules and biomicromolecules, so it is time-consuming and requires more samples. Thus, there is an urgent need for multichannel sensing systems that can simultaneously detect multiple biomacromolecules and biomicromolecules simultaneously. In recent years, light-addressable PEC sensors have been demonstrated to be a promising tool to achieve the high-throughput and simultaneous detection of multiple analytes. For example, Juan Wang et al. developed a light-addressable PEC sensor for the high-throughput biosensing of thrombin and the evaluation of inhibitor activity in serum samples [34]. Min Liu et al. reported a light-addressable paper-based PEC analytical device with tunable detection throughput for on-site biosensing of ultratrace organophosphorus pesticides [35]. These studies focused on the high-throughput of light-addressable PEC sensors and applied them into suitable sensing situations. Other studies on light-addressable PEC sensors emphasize its priority in detection multiple analytes. Juan Wang et al. developed a light-addressable PEC sensor for the sensitive detection of multiple DNA biomarkers on a single electrode [36]. Then, they reported a label-free and light-addressable PEC sensor and achieved the rapid and sensitive detection of multiple tumor markers on a single electrode [37]. Song Ma et al. constructed a light-addressable PEC sensor for multichannel detection of multiple biomicromolecules (dopamine and GSH), and applied it in probing Parkinson’s diseases [38]. To achieve an accurate diagnosis of pancreatic cancer, the simultaneous detection of biomacromolecules (GPC1 and CEA) and biomicromolecules (GSH) is of vital importance. Therefore, a novel light-addressable PEC sensor for the simultaneous detection of biomacromolecules and biomicromolecules was developed in this work.

To construct the light-addressable sensor, PEC sensors for CEA and GSH detection were fabricated. The PEC sensor for CEA detection was fabricated in the same way as the GPC1 sensor, but the anti-GPC1 was changed to anti-CEA. According to our previous work, carbon dots@mesoporous silica beads (CDs@MSB) showed excellent sensitivity to GSH by redox reaction, so CDs@MSB based PEC sensors were fabricated in this work to achieve ultrasensitive detection of GSH [27].

After PEC sensors for each biomarker were fabricated, they were combined and extended into a light-addressable sensing system. Then, the signal interferences in the mixed solution were studied to ensure that accurate concentrations of biomacromolecules and biomicromolecules were simultaneously obtained. Finally, the light-addressable PEC sensor in this paper were used to detect cell models, mouse models and human serum samples to evaluate its practicability and superiority. Through these detection results, the relationship between the concentration of GPC1, CEA and GSH and the stage of pancreatic cancer was also studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Materials and instruments
ce:section.ce:para: All chemical materials are listed in the Supplementary Information (SI). The absorption spectra of different nanomaterials were measured by a Maya2000 high-sensitivity spectrometer (Ocean Optics, USA). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of different nanomaterials were taken by Bruker Dimension Icon (Bruker, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of different nanomaterials were measured by a JEM-2800 (JEOL Ltd., Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis processes of different nanomaterials are described in SI. All nanomaterials were stored at 4 ℃ for later use. Prior to use, the nanomaterials were sonicated at 25 ℃ for 2 min

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of light-addressable PEC sensors and PEC measurement systems for multichannel detection of GPC1, CEA and GSH
Fabrication of PEC sensors for the detection of GPC1, CEA and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: The method of fabricating the PEC immunosensors for the detection of GPC1 and CEA is shown in Fig. S1. The AuNCs were self-assembled on the gold electrode by covalent bonding. Then, anti-GPC1 or anti-CEA was immobilized on the AuNCs. GPC1 or CEA can be captured by the specific binding between antibody and antigen. The fabrication process of the PEC based on redox reactions for the detection of GSH is shown in Fig. S2. CDs@MSB were self-assembled on the gold electrode by electrostatic bonding. Then, GSH can be detected by redox reactions between nanomaterials and GSH. More details of the fabrication processes of the sensors are elaborated in SI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the light-addressable PEC sensors and PEC measurement system
ce:section.ce:para: The PEC measurement system is shown in Fig. S3, and the details of each part are elucidated in SI. The structure of homemade light-addressable multichannel sensor is shown in Fig. 1A. Three fabricated PEC sensors were combined with the conductive tape and fixed on one homemade PEC cell. The light source can be moved by motorized translation stage to illuminate the three sensor chips successively. When one of the three sensor chip was excited by the light source, the external circuit can obtain the photocurrent signal. Then the light source was moved by the motorized translation stage to another sensor chip to obtain a new photocurrent signal, as shown in Fig. 1B. Finally, the three analytes can be detected by this process. In the future, more PEC sensors can be connected to detect more biochemical indices for the detection of different diseases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pancreatic cancer biological model
ce:section.ce:para: Cell lines including human pancreatic cancer cells (PANC-1) and human dermal fibroblasts (HDFs) were cultured in DMEM high-glucose medium and DMEM/F12 medium respectively as cell models. Male Balb/c-nu mice were fed after the injection of pancreatic cancer cells, and blood samples were collected as mouse model. The blood samples of patients with pancreatic cancer were collected as patient models. More details of the procedures of biological models are shown in SI. Animal experiments were approved by the Animal Experiments Ethical Committee of Nankai University and complied with the Guide for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals. This study was approved by the Institutional Ethics Review Board of Tianjin Medical University Cancer Institute and Hospital, with written informed consents obtained from each patient.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussions
Characterization of different nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption spectra of AuNCs and AuNCs/GO are shown in Fig. 2A, and the absorption spectra of CDs and CDs@MSB are shown in Fig. 2B. From these absorption spectra, the introduction of GO and MSB did not cause a shift in the absorption peak. The absorption peak of AuNCs/GO and CDs@MSB appeared at 373 nm and 351 nm respectively. Therefore, a 365 nm LED light source was selected to excite different nanomaterial-based PEC sensors simultaneously, which simplified the light-addressable experiment to achieve the detection of various biomolecules. Then, TEM and AFM were used to characterize the surface morphology of the nanocomposites. The TEM images of different nanomaterials are shown in Fig. S4. The size distribution of AuNCs in Fig. S4B shows that the average diameter of AuNCs was approximately 4.5 nm. The inserted image in Fig. S4A was a TEM image with higher resolution, which confirms the morphology of AuNCs. The TEM images indicate that AuNCs was successfully synthesized. Fig. S4C shows that the foliated GO had a maximum diameter over 500 nm. According to the AFM images of AuNCs and GO, the heights of AuNCs and GO were approximately 5.5 nm and 1.7 nm respectively. The AFM images of AuNCs/GO are shown in Fig. 2C. It can be seen that the height of AuNCs/GO consisted of two parts: 1.7 nm (GO) and 5.5 nm (AuNCs). The height of AuNCs (5.5 nm) in AFM image was a bit larger than the size of AuNCs (4.5 nm) in TEM image. This is because AFM can characterize the height of dried ligands (MUA) on the surface of Au cores of AuNCs, while TEM can only characterize the Au cores of AuNCs. From Fig. S5C, we can see that the height of CDs was approximately 2 nm. According to Fig. S4D, the average diameter of MSB was 230 nm. As shown in Fig. 2D, the height of CDs@MSB was 230 nm. It confirmed that CDs were on the pores of the MSB, which was consistent with the literatures[27,39]. All these results confirmed that these nanomaterials were well synthesized and well dispersed on the electrodes. Therefore, the PEC sensors can be developed by using these nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AuNCs/GO based PEC immunosensors for the detection of GPC1 and CEA
Characteristics of the PEC immunosensor during fabrication and sensing process
ce:section.ce:para: The photoelectric conversion process of AuNCs based PEC sensor is elucidated in the SI. The photocurrent of AuNCs based PEC sensors in PBS at different potentials was shown in Fig. 3A. The results show that AuNCs have photoelectric conversion capability, so they can be used to fabricate PEC sensors. Then, GO was introduced into the AuNCs as hybrid nanomaterials to improve the photoelectric conversion capability, the mechanism of which has been studied in previous studies, as shown in Fig. 3B [28]. There are many carboxyl groups on the surface of AuNCs, which enables reactions with the amino groups of the antibody by covalent binding [39]. Once anti-GPC1 was decorated on the surface of AuNCs/GO/AuE PEC through covalent binding, GPC1 can be captured. Then, the impedance of the PEC sensor increased and the photocurrent output decreased because of the hindrance and insulation property of GPC1.

To confirm the fabrication and sensing process of the PEC immunosensor, the photocurrent response of the modified electrodes was recorded at different stages, as shown in Fig. 3C. The GO/AuE had an ultralow photocurrent response, which indicated that GO itself did not generate photocurrent. After 0.1 mg/mL GO solution was introduced into AuNCs to form AuNCs/GO nanocomposites, they were self-assembled on AuE to fabricate AuNCs/GO/AuE. Compared with that of AuNCs/AuE, the photocurrent response of AuNCs/GO/AuE increased sharply to 180 nA. This result proved that the introduction of GO has greatly improved the photoelectric conversion property of the nanomaterials. After AuNCs/GO/AuE were incubated in PBS containing 10 μg/mL anti-GPC1 for 1.5 h, anti-GPC1 was modified onto nanocomposites, and then anti-GPC1/AuNCs/GO/AuE was immersed in 10 mg/mL bovine serum albumin (BSA) to block the nonspecific binding sites on the electrode surface [40–42]. It can be seen that the photocurrent of anti-GPC1/AuNCs/GO/AuE decreased (curve d). A further drop can be observed after the detection of 5 ng/mL GPC1 in PBS (curve e). Protein molecules such as anti-GPC1 and GPC1 are insulted, thus preventing the transfer process of photoinduced electron-hole pairs [43,44]. Therefore, the successive reduction of the photocurrent after anti-GPC1 and GPC1 binding with the sensor chip demonstrates the feasibility of the PEC immunosensor for the detection of GPC1.

To further verify the fabrication and sensing process of the PEC immunosensor, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out by potentiostat in a solution of 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. The EIS Nyquist plots of the sensor at different stages are shown in Fig. 3D. The high-frequency semicircle in the Nyquist plot represents the R et, which can reflect the immobilization situation on the modified electrode interface. The impedance of AuNCs/GO/AuE was much smaller than that of AuNCs/AuE, because GO improved the electron transfer of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- at the electrode surface. When the anti-GPC1 was attached to the AuNCs, the Nyquist plot of anti-GPC1/AuNCs/GO/AuE showed a much larger half-circle diameter than AuNCs/GO/AuE because of the hindrance and insulation property of protein [45,46]. In general, these results demonstrated that the proposed PEC sensor has been successfully fabricated and could be applied for the determination of GPC1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced sensing properties by the optimization of nanocomposites and measurement conditions
ce:section.ce:para: According to previous studies, the sensing properties of PEC immunosensors mainly depend on the photoelectric performance and the biological reaction performance between the antigen and antibody [22,49,50]. The photoelectric performance of the PEC immunosensor was mainly related to the photoelectric conversion capability of nanomaterials and their applied potentials. Meanwhile, biological reaction performance depends on the sensitivity of specific biological reactions between the antibody and antigen.

To improve the photoelectric performance of the immunosensor, some experimental conditions were optimized and the mechanism of the GO enhancing sensing property was studied. First, the influence of the applied potential on photocurrent is shown in Fig. S7A. It can be seen that the photocurrent increased with larger potentials. This can be attributed to the stronger driving force for the electron transfer at higher potentials [28,39]. Thus, at higher potentials, the sensor can provide larger basal photocurrent for GPC1 detection. Then, photocurrent reduction when detecting GPC1 at different potentials was shown in Fig. 4A. The results indicated that the photocurrent reduction of the PEC immunosensor became larger with the increasing applied potential when detecting GPC1. It should be noted that the reductions in photocurrent at negative potentials were near zero, which were much smaller than those at positive potentials. There are two main reasons for this. Firstly, the impedance change caused by GPC1 connecting to the electrode is a same value at different potentials. Therefore, the larger the basal photocurrent is, the more the photocurrent decreases. As shown in Fig. S7A, the photocurrent of the PEC sensor at negative potentials was much smaller than that at positive potentials. Therefore, the decrease in photocurrent at negative potentials was much smaller. Second, the isoelectric point of GPC1 is in the pH range of 5–7. The pH of PBS is 7.4, so GPC1 carries a weak negative charge in the solution. At negative potentials, the electrode surface will provide a weak repulsive force to GPC1, leading to a more difficult binding process for GPC1. Thus, under the combined influence of the two factors, the photocurrent drop caused by GPC1 at negative potentials was much smaller than that at positive potentials. Based on the results above, it can be concluded that the PEC immunosensor can achieve a better sensitivity at higher applied potentials where the sensor exhibited better photoelectric performance. However, too high potentials will cause a greater drift and deviation of the photocurrent of the sensor and increase the interferences of other coexisting species in the sample [51]. Thus, 500 mV was finally chosen as the optimized potential for the PEC immunosensor in the following experiments to obtain better sensing properties.

Next, the relationship between the photoelectric property and the GO concentration used in fabricating AuNCs/GO nanocomposites was studied. First, the photocurrent of AuNCs/AuE and AuNCs/GO/AuE was compared at different potentials. The results showed that AuNCs/GO/AuE exhibited larger photocurrent amplitude than AuNCs/AuE at different potentials. This improvement in photoelectric property originated from GO that served as an electron acceptor. GO can capture electrons from AuNCs and then reduce the recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs, so more photoinduced electron-hole pairs can be involved in PEC reactions. Then, the concentration of GO was optimized. As shown in Fig. 4B, when the concentration of GO increased from 0.02 m g/mL to 0.1 mg/mL, the photocurrent in PBS increased from 140.535 nA to 188.386 nA. It indicated that increased GO concentration contributed to more enhanced photoelectric properties. However, the photocurrent exhibited an obvious downward tendency when the GO concentration increased from 0.1 mg/mL to 0.4 mg/mL. Excessive GO can absorb part of the illumination, which inhibits the excitation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs of AuNCs. Meanwhile, excessive GO increased the impedance of the entire system [38,39]. These two reasons together led to the decline in photocurrent. Therefore, the optimal concentration of GO was 0.1 mg/mL. The improvement in photoelectric properties can contribute to better sensing properties. The relationship between the sensitivity and concentration of GO was studied at a potential of 500 mV when the GPC1 concentration increased from 0.01 to 5 ng/mL. As shown in Fig. 4C, the sensitivity of the PEC immunosensor was much better after GO was introduced to form AuNCs/GO nanocomposites. Additionally, the sensitivity of the sensor showed a similar upward and then downward trend as concentration of GO increased, which was consistent with the enhancement of photoelectric property by GO. This further confirmed that the improvement in photoelectric properties contributed to the sensing properties of the PEC immunosensor. The optimal concentration of GO was 0.1 mg/mL, where the corresponding sensitivity of the sensor was 5.504 nA/decade. It should be noted that GO can improve the sensitivity not only by enhancing the photoelectric property, but also by affecting the biological reaction performance between antibody and antigen. A previous study has demonstrated that GO can increase the antibody loading property of PEC sensors to improve the sensitivity of PEC immunosensors [39]. It suggests that more antibodies can be modified on AuNCs/GO nanocomposites than on AuNCs.

To confirm that AuNCs/GO nanocomposites can improve the antibody loading property for anti-GPC1, EIS was used to study the impedance of PEC immunosensors fabricated by AuNCs and AuNCs/GO after they were incubated with anti-GPC1 for the same amount of time. The results are shown in Fig. S7B. Compared with anti-GPC1/AuNCs/AuE (curve a), the Nyquist plot of anti-GPC1/AuNCs/GO/AuE (curve b) showed a much larger half-circle diameter, which suggested a larger Ret. This result indicated that more anti-GPC1 was modified on the PEC sensor after the introduction of GO. The improved anti-GPC1 loading property can be attributed to the abundant carboxyl groups of GO [39]. This improvement in the anti-GPC1 loading property can also be confirmed by the photocurrent response change of AuNCs/AuE and AuNCs/GO/AuE after incubation with anti-GPC1. As shown in Fig. S7C, the photocurrent of anti-GPC1/AuNCs/AuE decreased by 22.491 nA compared with that of AuNCs/AuE. The photocurrent response of anti-GPC1/AuNCs/GO/AuE decreased by 89.844 nA compared with that of AuNCs/GO/AuE, as shown in Fig. S7D. From the larger decline in photocurrent of anti-GPC1/AuNCs/GO/AuE than anti-GPC1/AuNCs/AuE, it can be concluded that when GO was introduced into AuNCs, more anti-GPC1 can be modified on the PEC sensor. When detecting GPC1, the more anti-GPC1 is on the sensor, the more GPC1 will be captured. Therefore, after GO was introduced to form AuNCs/GO nanocomposites, more anti-GPC1 can be modified on the nanomaterials to improve the sensitivity. Based on these results, it can be concluded that by introducing GO into AuNCs as hybrid nanomaterials, GO can not only improve the photoelectric properties, but also increase the biological reaction performance between the antibody and antigen, to improve the sensing properties for the detection of GPC1.

To further study the effect of the introduction of GO, the percentage of increase in photocurrent and sensitivity of the sensor with different GO concentrations is shown in Fig. S7E. The percentage of increase in sensitivity varied with increasing photocurrent, demonstrating that changes in photocurrent affect changes in sensitivity. When the GO concentration increased from 0.02 mg/mL to 0.1 mg/mL, the increase in sensitivity was less than the increase in photocurrent, indicating that the improved antibody loading properties by GO were not sufficient to fully exploit the increased photocurrent at this time. However, when the GO concentration further increased, the percentage of increase in photocurrent was less than that in sensitivity. This indicates that the increased antibody loading property by GO has a dominant status in improving sensitivity at this moment. Based on these results, it can be concluded that the enhanced sensitivity of the PEC immunosensor is simultaneously influenced by both the photoelectric performance and the antibody loading property.

In addition, to improve the sensing performance, several relevant experimental conditions, including the incubation time when detecting GPC1 and the incubation time and volume of anti-GPC1 when fabricating the PEC immunosensor, were also optimized. The results are shown in Fig. S8 and discussed in SI. Under the optimal conditions, the sensitivity of the PEC immunosensor was 7.265 nA/decade, and the LOD was 15 pg/mL. The relevant working curve of the PEC immunosensor for GPC1 detection was shown in Fig. 4D.

The fabrication of the PEC immunosensor for CEA detection was similar to that of the PEC immunosensor for GPC detection, and the anti-CEA/AuNCs/GO/AuE PEC sensor was optimized using a similar method. Based on the optimization experiments of GPC1 detection, + 500 mV was chosen as the working potential and the incubation time for anti-CEA was determined to be 3.5 h. Then, the concentrations of anti-CEA and GO were optimized. The results are shown in Fig. S9. After the optimization of the concentrations of anti-CEA and GO, the PEC immunosensor for CEA detection obtained an optimal sensitivity of 7.616 nA/decade and the best LOD (51 pg/mL) in the linear range of CEA concentrations from 0.01 to 5 ng/mL.

In addition, the selectivity of the PEC immunosensors for the detection of GPC1 and CEA was studied by measuring different interferences. The results are shown in Figs. S10 and S11, which demonstrate the excellent selectivity of the sensors.

The PEC sensor for GPC1 detection was compared with other representative papers for GPC1 detection in serum, and the result was shown in Table S1 in SI. It can be seen from Table S1 that even compared with the optical and electrochemical methods which are usually highly sensitive, the PEC sensor of this work has better LOD. Also, the LOD is better than the ones using enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and comparable with single-molecule assay which require expensive equipment and complicated operations. Thus, we can conclude that the PEC sensor in this work is a promising tool for GPC1 detection in blood and can meet the requirement for early diagnosis of pancreatic cancer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs@MSB based PEC sensor for GSH detection
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing mechanism for GSH was based on redox reaction, which is elucidated by Fig. 5A. The photoelectric conversion process of CDs was discussed in SI. When MSB is introduced to CDs to form CDs@MSB nanocomposites, the pores on the MSB provide places for CDs to adhere. Thus, the introduction of MSB provide larger surface-to-volume ratio for more CDs connecting to the electrode. Therefore, the photoelectric property of the sensor will be improved. Besides, CDs can be effectively protected from aggregation and the contact area between CDs and GSH significantly will increase, which enhance their catalytic stabilities and properties to GSH [27]. By improving the photoelectric properties and catalytic properties simultaneously, the introduction of MSB can significantly enhance the sensing properties of the PEC sensor to GSH.

The photocurrent change of CDs@MSB based PEC sensor had a linear relationship with GSH concentration, and the slope was defined as the sensitivity of the sensor. According to previous work, CDs@MSB based PEC sensor showed a better sensitivity at the potential of 500 mV [27]. This potential was the same with the AuNCs based PEC sensor for the detection of GPC1 and CEA. Therefore, to achieve high sensing properties and simple operation of the multichannel detection, 500 mV was selected as the optimal potential for the detection of GSH. Then, the concentration of MSB was optimized. The influence of MSB concentration on the photocurrent and sensitivity of the PEC sensor was shown in Fig. 5B and C. This result indicated that the PEC sensor showed better sensitivity when the MSB concentration was 1.5 μM. The decrease in photocurrent and sensitivity when the concentration of MSB is high can be explained by two reasons: the absorption of illumination and the high impedance caused by excessive MSB. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the photocurrent of the CDs@MSB based GSH PEC sensor in solution containing different concentrations of GSH is shown in Fig. 5D. The sensor showed high sensitivity (21.42 nA/μM) and low LOD (34.9 nM) at 500 mV. In addition, the PEC sensor for GSH detection also showed excellent selectivity, which is shown in Fig. S10.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Light-addressable PEC sensors for multichannel detection of biomacromolecules and biomicromolecules
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the multichannel detection of GPC1, CEA and GSH, different PEC sensors were combined and extended into light-addressable sensors. It should be noted that the mechanism and enhancing strategy of detecting biomacromolecules and biomicromolecules are different. The mechanism for biomacromolecules is based on impedance, and the enhancing strategy is based on improving the electron transfer and antibody loading property by GO. The mechanism for biomicromolecules is based on redox reactions, and the enhancing strategy is attributed to improving the catalytic properties and the surface-to-volume ratio for CDs connecting to the electrode by MSB. To study the interferences between the detection of biomacromolecules and biomicromolecules, the photocurrents of the light-addressable sensor when detecting GPC1 and GSH were observed in mixed solutions containing different concentrations of GPC1 and GSH, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6A, the PEC immunosensor for GPC1 detection exhibited the same photocurrent response in solution containing different concentrations of GSH. This result indicated that the presence of GSH did not affect the ultrasensitive detection of GPC1 because the sensing mechanism was based on the specific binding process between the antibody and antigen. Similarly, as shown in Fig. 6B, the presence of GPC1 in the solution did not affect the detection of GSH because the mechanism was based on redox reactions and GPC1 could not be oxidized by photoexcited holes. Compared with the reported multichannel detection of small biomolecules in previous research[38], there was much less interference between different analytes in this work because they were based on different mechanisms. Hence, it was feasible to detect biomacromolecules and biomicromolecules simultaneously by light-addressable sensors. Based on impedance and redox reactions respectively, the detections for biomacromolecules and biomicromolecules do not interfere with each other, so concentrations of GPC1, CEA and GSH can be measured simultaneously.

We also summarized the strategies used by PEC sensors to inhibit mutual interference between multiple analytes reported in recent years, the result was shown in Table S2 in SI. Attributed to well-designed sensing and the related enhancing mechanisms for different analytes, our light-addressable sensor inhibits mutual interference during sensing process successfully. Compared with other sensors in the recent years, the sensor in this work is more advantageous because it did not require extra process in hardware (potentiostat with different potentials, light sources with different wavelengths) or software (computer programs or algorithm) to inhibit the interference among multiple analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preliminary applications of the light-addressable PEC sensor in the diagnosis of pancreatic cancer
The detection results of GPC1, CEA and GSH in the cell model
ce:section.ce:para: To preliminarily verify the feasibility of the light-addressable sensors, they were used to detect GPC1, CEA and GSH in different numbers of PANC-1 cells, with HDFs as control groups. Before the samples were detected by the light-addressable PEC sensor, calibration curves were obtained. The cell samples in Fig. 7 were obtained in radioimmunoprecipitation assay (RIPA) buffer, so RIPA buffer containing different concentrations of GPC1, CEA and GSH was prepared. Then, the corresponding calibration curve for the three analytes was obtained by observing the photocurrent response of the light-addressable PEC sensor in these solutions. The results are shown in Fig. S12. Then, the samples from different cell samples were detected by the light-addressable PEC sensor, Meanwhile, the same cell samples were detected by commercial kits as a reference. From the detection results by light-addressable sensors shown in Fig. 7A, B and C, we can see that the concentration of GPC1, CEA and GSH increased with an increasing number of PANC-1 cells and HDF cells. Compared with those from the same number of HDF cells, the concentrations of GPC1, CEA and GSH from PANC-1 cells were obviously higher, which was in line with the trend of cancer development. The results of the kits shown in Fig. 7D, E and F were almost the same as those of the light-addressable sensor, but the error bar values of the results were much larger than those measured by the light-addressable sensor. These results firmly demonstrated the feasibility and practicality of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection results of GPC1, CEA and GSH in the mouse model
ce:section.ce:para: To further verify the practicality in complex biological environment, the light-addressable sensor was applied to detect GPC1, CEA and GSH in serum samples from a mouse model. The calibration curves for the three analytes were obtained by observing the photocurrent response of the light-addressable PEC sensor in mouse serum containing different concentrations of GPC1, CEA and GSH. The calibrations are shown in Fig. S13. As shown in Fig. 8A, B and C, the concentrations of GPC1, CEA and GSH from the mouse models at different pancreatic cancer stages were higher than those obtained from the control groups (mice cultured for the same days without injection of pancreatic cancer cells). With the development of pancreatic cancer, GPC1 and GSH showed an obvious increase, while there was no significant difference among the concentrations of CEA in mouse models cultured for different times. This result demonstrated that GPC1 provides higher sensitivity for pancreatic cancer, which was consistent with results of previous studies [52,53]. The results of the kits are shown in Fig. 8D, E and F. These results exhibited the same trend as those detected by the multichannel PEC sensing system, which demonstrated the feasibility of the light-addressable sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection results of GPC1, CEA and GSH in the patient model
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, after preliminary testing in cell models and mouse models, light-addressable sensors were used to study the concentrations of GPC1, CEA and GSH in patient models at different stages of pancreatic cancer. The calibration curves for the three analytes were obtained by observing the photocurrent response of the light-addressable PEC sensor in human serum containing different concentrations of GPC1, CEA and GSH. The calibrations are shown in Fig. S14. The detection results by light-addressable sensors and commercial kits are shown in Fig. 9. As shown in Fig. 9A and C, there were significant differences in the concentrations of GPC1 and GSH in patients at different stages of pancreatic cancer. However, the concentration of CEA only increased slightly with the development of pancreatic cancer, as shown in Fig. 9B. This confirmed that sensitivity of CEA for the early diagnosis of pancreatic cancer was much lower than that of GPC1, which was consistent with previous studies [52,53].

The results from the kits are shown in Fig. 9D, E and F. The tendencies of the analytes measured by the kits were consistent with those measured by the light-addressable sensor, whose feasibility and practicality were confirmed. Compared with kits, the light-addressable sensor showed much higher sensitivities. Meanwhile, PEC sensors have much smaller error bars for the detection results in blood samples, which was attributed to their strong resistance to interference in complex biological environments. In addition, the light-addressable sensor has a fast response and simple operations.

We compared the combined biomarker analysis for pancreatic cancer diagnosis in recent years, the result was shown in SI (Table S3). Compared with other methods, the light-addressable sensor in this work can achieve simultaneous highly-sensitive detection of multiple biomarkers of pancreatic cancer. The whole process is within 5 min without any pretreatment process thereby being convenient than other common methods (Table S3). Therefore, the light-addressable PEC sensor was demonstrated to be a promising tool for rapid combined biomarker analysis for early diagnosis of pancreatic cancer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, a light-addressable PEC sensor was developed for the simultaneous detection of biomacromolecules and biomicromolecules. High-sensitivity and multichannel detection of GPC1, CEA and GSH was achieved by the light-addressable PEC sensor and was applied in the evaluation and early diagnosis of pancreatic cancer.

PEC immunosensors based on the impedance mechanism were fabricated by AuNCs for the detection of GPC1 for the first time. To improve the sensitivity, GO was introduced into AuNCs as a hybrid nanomaterial. The results showed that GO improved the sensitivity by enhancing both the photoelectric properties and antibody loading properties. After optimization, AuNCs/GO based PEC immunosensors achieved a high sensitivity (7.265 nA/decade) and good LOD (15 pg/mL) in a linear range of 0.01–5 ng/mL for the detection of GPC1. Then, the PEC immunosensor for CEA detection was fabricated using the same process as that for GPC1 detection, and finally achieved an optimal sensitivity of 7.616 nA/decade. The PEC sensor based on the redox reaction was fabricated by CDs@MSB for the detection of GSH. The introduction of MSB to CDs improved the photoelectric properties and catalytic performances of the sensor. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the CDs@MSB based GSH PEC sensor showed high sensitivity (21.42 nA/μM) and a low LOD (34.9 nM) in the linear range of 0–1.5 μM.

Then, PEC sensors for the detection of GPC1, CEA and GSH were combined and extended into light-addressable sensors for multichannel detection. The light addressable sensor showed high specificity and sensitivity. Meanwhile, there were few interferences among the detections of biomacromolecules and those of biomicromolecules because they were based on impedance mechanism and redox mechanism respectively. Finally, the light-addressable sensor was used to detect GPC1, CEA and GSH in cell, mouse and patient models to verify its accuracy and practicality in the diagnosis of pancreatic cancer. The results showed that GPC1, CEA and GSH can be detected accurately and simultaneously by the light-addressable sensor. Compared with commercial kits, the light-addressable sensor has a higher sensitivity, lower detection limit and faster detection speed. In particular, the light-addressable sensor exhibits much stronger anti-interference ability in complex biological environments. In the future, the light-addressable sensor can be expanded to measure more biomarkers to diagnose pancreatic cancer more accurately and sensitively.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Gang Xiao: He finished the master degree of Electronic Science and Technology from Nankai University, Tianjin, China, in 2021. He majored in the nanomaterial-based photoelectrochemical sensor and the development of embedded system. He focused on multichannel PEC sensors and reusable photoelectrochemical sensor based on Fe3O4 nanoparticles for real-time glucose detection with high sensitivity.

Haize Ge: MD, PHD, graduated from Tianjin Medical University in 2015, his major is pathology and pathophysiology. After graduation, has been working in the clinical laboratory of Tianjin Third Central hospital . His research interests include metabolomics, genomics and proteomics of liver, pancreas and other related tissues.

Qiaochun Yang: She finished the master degree of Electronic Science and Technology from Nankai University, Tianjin, China, in 2021. She majored in the nanomaterial-based photoelectrochemical sensor and its application in cancer diagnosis.
Zhen Zhang: She is currently a master student majoring in the construction of tissue engineering and artificial blood vessel by biological method in Nankai University, Tianjin, China.

Linyang Cheng: She is currently a master student majoring in the photoelectrochemical sensor at Nankai University, Tianjin, China. Her researches are mainly based on the biosensors and intelligent circuit systems, such as the PEC aptamer sensor for MC-LR detection.

Shengli Cao: He received the master degree of Electronics and Communication Engineering from Taiyuan University of Technology, China, in 2020. He is currently pursuing his Ph.D. degree in Nankai University, Tianjin, China. During master degree, his researches included the self-powered sensing systems and energy harvesting. Currently, He majors in Electronic Science and Technology focusing on the nanomaterial-based photoelectrochemical biological sensors.

Jing Ji: received his B.Sc. degree in Microelectronic Engineering from Nankai University, China, in 2003, and his MS and Ph.D. degrees from Waseda University, Japan in 2007 and 2013, respectively. From 2013 to 2016 He worked as a research fellow in Information, Production and Systems Research Center, Waseda University, Japan. Since 2016, he has been an associated professor at Xidian University, China. He is a senior member of Chinese Society of Micro Nano Technology(CSMNT), and a member of the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE), the Institute of Electrical Engineers of Japan (IEEJ), the Japan Society of Applied Physics (JSAP) and the Society of Instrument and Control Engineers of Japan(SICE). His research area includes: MEMS devices, and sensing technology.
Jun Zhang: He received his master degree in Nankai University in 2004 and doctor degree in Nankai University in 2007. 2007–2010, he finished his post-doctoral research in UCLA.

Zhao Yue: He received the Ph.D. degree in Microelectronics from Nankai University, Tianjin, China, in 2009. He was a lecturer of Microelectronics from 2009 to 2011, and then associate professor from 2011 to 2017 in Nankai University. During 2015–2016, he was also a visiting scholar in the Department of Physics at the University of Marburg, Germany. He is currently a professor in the Department of Microelectronics, Nankai University. His research interests include nanosensors, semiconductor sensors, biochips, low power portable intelligent sensing platform, multi-sensor integrated hardware system, and software system for multi-sensor signal processing
